rationale: This page contains detailed information on crocodile conservation in India, including specific species profiles (Saltwater Crocodile, Mugger, and Gharial), their IUCN/CITES/WPA status, biological characteristics, and geographical habitats. It is highly suitable for factual and comparative questions.
cleanedText:
o Tikarpada (Satkosia) for gharials; Dangamal (Bhitarkanika) for saltwater crocodiles; Ramatirtha (Similipal) for mugger crocodiles.
• Saltwater Crocodile & Mugger Recovery:
o Saltwater crocodile population: Recovered to ~2,500 individuals
o Mugger crocodile population: Rebounded to 8,000–10,000 individuals, reclaiming most of its historical range (i.e., Ganga River drainage).
• Gharial Conservation: National Chambal Sanctuary (spanning Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh), Katarnia Ghat Wildlife Sanctuary (Uttar Pradesh), Gandak River, Corbett Tiger Reserve (Uttarakhand), and Son Gharial Sanctuary (Madhya Pradesh).
o India now holds 80% of the global wild gharial population.
Ongoing Conservation Efforts:
• Gharial Conservation Project (2025), aimed to expand gharial populations across the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Indus rivers, as well as Mahanadi in Odisha.
• Madras Crocodile Bank: Breeding crocodiles and supporting reintroduction programmes.
### Crocodiles Found in India
#### 1. Saltwater Crocodile (Estuarine Crocodile) (Crocodylus porosus)
- IUCN Status: LC (Least Concern)
- CITES: Appendix I
- WPA, 1972: Schedule I
#### 2. Marsh Crocodile (Mugger, Broad-snouted Crocodile) (Crocodylus palustris)
- IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable)
- CITES: Appendix I
- WPA, 1972: Schedule I
#### 3. Gharial (Fish Eating Crocodile, Indian Gavial, Long Nosed Crocodile) (Gavialis gangeticus)
- IUCN Status: CR (Critically Endangered)
- CITES: Appendix I
- WPA, 1972: Schedule I
### Characteristics
- Nocturnal animals.
- Poikilothermic (regulate their body temperature only to a limited degree).
- Diversity: 13 different crocodile species, 3 native species found in India:
- Saltwater crocodile: Largest crocodile and Earth's largest living vertebrate-class reptile. Strongest bite force of any living animal.
- Muggers: Known to use 'tools' e.g. balance stick on their head to lure birds for nesting material. Only Indian species that digs burrows on land to escape extreme temperatures.
- Gharial: Adult males have a bulb-like structure on snout tip called GHARA (acts as a resonator to produce loud buzzing noises and blow bubbles to attract females). Expert fish catchers. Thinnest and most elongated snout among crocodilians. Only visibly sexually dimorphic crocodilian.
### Habitat
- Saltwater crocodile: Brackish water, mangrove swamps, estuaries, and coastal waters.
- Locations: Bhitarkanika in Odisha (largest share), Andaman & Nicobar Islands and Sundarbans (West Bengal).
- Muggers: Freshwater habitats like lakes, rivers, and slow-moving streams.
- Gharial: Deep, clear, fast-flowing freshwater rivers with high sandbanks for nesting.
- Nepal: Rapti-Narayani River.
- India (Ganges tributaries): Girwa (UP), Son (MP), Ramganga (Uttarakhand), Gandak (Bihar), Chambal (UP, MP, Rajasthan), Mahanadi (Odisha).
